summary: Gruber analyzes Google's acquisition of Nest, focusing on what Tony Fadell brings to Google beyond thermostats and smoke detectors. He highlights Fadell's proven track record at Apple with the iPod — not just in hardware design but in scaling production and iterating year after year. Gruber invokes Alan Kay's famous dictum about serious software companies making their own hardware, noting that Google has historically only done this for server infrastructure, not consumer products. He argues the acquisition is less about Nest's specific products and more about Google finally gaining the capability to do consumer hardware well. The financial resources solve Fadell's scaling bottleneck while giving Google something it has always lacked.
key_points: Fadell's value isn't just hardware design — it's his proven ability to scale production and iterate on consumer products year after year
Google has historically followed Alan Kay's hardware dictum only for server infrastructure, never for consumer devices
The acquisition is strategically about Google acquiring consumer hardware competence, not about thermostats specifically
Money alone doesn't solve scaling problems, but solving scaling problems always requires money — making Google's resources the key enabler for Nest
money_quotes: He also knows scale — creating devices that sell tens of millions of units.
Not just building and shipping an iPod, but building and shipping new iPods year after year, each better, in some way, than the last.
People who are really serious about software should partner with an OEM in Asia. No, wait, that's not what he said.
People who are really serious about software should make their own hardware. That's never been true of Google.
The software that Google was most serious about — web search, Gmail, and so forth — ran in the cloud, and with the company's legendary data centers, they effectively built their own hardware.
Money doesn't solve scaling problems, but the actual solutions to scaling problems always cost money.
Google's Nest acquisition has very little to do with selling thermostats and smoke detectors in particular. Instead, it's about Google having the ability to do consumer hardware right, in general.
key_insight: The Nest acquisition isn't about Google buying a thermostat company — it's about Google finally acquiring the consumer hardware expertise and iteration discipline it has always lacked.
